concepts.name: Development
concepts.description: Moving pieces from their starting squares to active squares as quickly as possible. Rapid development creates threats and controls key squares early in the game.

concepts.name: Central Control
concepts.description: Occupying or controlling the central squares (e4, d4, e5, d5) with pawns and pieces. Central control provides mobility, flexibility, and the ability to shift attacks to either flank.

concepts.name: Open Files
concepts.description: A file with no pawns of either colour. Rooks and queens gain maximum activity on open files, especially when aimed at the opponent's back rank or weak pawns.

concepts.name: Semi-Open File
concepts.description: A file containing only the opponent's pawn(s). Semi-open files are natural homes for rooks to apply pressure against the opponent's pawn structure.

concepts.name: Weak Squares
concepts.description: Squares that can no longer be defended by pawns, making them ideal outposts for enemy pieces. Recognizing and exploiting weak squares is a hallmark of positional play.

concepts.name: Outpost
concepts.description: A square on the fourth, fifth, or sixth rank that is protected by a friendly pawn and cannot be attacked by enemy pawns. Knights are especially powerful on outposts.

concepts.name: Minority Attack
concepts.description: Advancing pawns on the side where you have fewer pawns to create weaknesses in the opponent's pawn majority. A classic strategy in the Queen's Gambit Declined and Carlsbad structures.

concepts.name: Isolated Queen's Pawn
concepts.description: A d-pawn with no friendly pawns on the adjacent c- and e-files. The IQP grants dynamic piece activity and central space but can become a weakness in endgames.

concepts.name: Hanging Pawns
concepts.description: A pair of adjacent pawns on the fourth rank (typically c4+d4 or c5+d5) with no pawns on adjacent files. They control space but can be targets if immobilised.

concepts.name: Fianchetto
concepts.description: Developing a bishop to g2 or b2 (or g7/b7 for Black) after advancing the g- or b-pawn one square. The fianchettoed bishop controls a long diagonal and supports central play from the flank.

concepts.name: Kingside Expansion
concepts.description: Advancing pawns on the kingside (f, g, h files) to gain space and create attacking chances against the enemy king. Must be timed carefully to avoid weakening one's own king.

concepts.name: Exchange Sacrifice
concepts.description: Deliberately giving up a rook for a minor piece (bishop or knight) to gain positional compensation such as a strong outpost, pawn structure advantage, or attacking chances.

concepts.name: Piece Activity
concepts.description: The principle that active, well-placed pieces are more valuable than passive ones. Improving piece activity is often more important than material considerations.

concepts.name: King Safety
concepts.description: Keeping the king protected, typically by castling early and maintaining a solid pawn shield. Neglecting king safety invites tactical attacks and mating threats.

concepts.name: Pawn Structure
concepts.description: The arrangement of pawns on the board, which dictates the character of the position: where pieces belong, which files to open, and whether the game is open or closed.

concepts.name: Castling
concepts.description: A special king move that simultaneously improves king safety and connects the rooks. Castling kingside (O-O) is more common; queenside (O-O-O) is often associated with aggressive play.

concepts.name: Tempo
concepts.description: A unit of time in chess measured in moves. Gaining a tempo means achieving a useful result (like developing a piece) while forcing the opponent to waste a move. Losing tempi in the opening can be fatal.

concepts.name: Initiative
concepts.description: The ability to create threats and dictate the pace of the game. The player with the initiative forces the opponent to react rather than execute their own plans.

concepts.name: Pawn Storm
concepts.description: Rapidly advancing pawns toward the opponent's king to open lines and create mating attacks. Often seen when players castle on opposite sides.

concepts.name: Bishop Pair
concepts.description: Having both bishops when the opponent has traded one or both. The bishop pair is especially powerful in open positions, where the long-range bishops can dominate.

concepts.name: Pin
concepts.description: A tactical motif where a piece is immobilised because moving it would expose a more valuable piece behind it to capture. Absolute pins (against the king) prevent the pinned piece from moving at all.

concepts.name: Fork
concepts.description: A tactic where a single piece attacks two or more enemy pieces simultaneously. Knight forks are especially common and dangerous due to the knight's unique movement.

concepts.name: Discovered Attack
concepts.description: An attack revealed when a piece moves out of the way of another piece's line of attack. Discovered checks are especially powerful as they give the moving piece a free move.

concepts.name: Rook Activity
concepts.description: Placing rooks on open or semi-open files, or on the seventh rank, where they exert maximum influence. Active rooks are crucial for converting advantages in the middlegame and endgame.

concepts.name: Space Advantage
concepts.description: Controlling more territory on the board by advancing pawns beyond the fourth rank. A space advantage gives pieces more room to manoeuvre and restricts the opponent's options.
